title: Active and passive citizens: a defense of majoritarian democracy [Book Review]
author: Abolafia

opening_move.description: The review opens by framing Tuck's book as a Rousseau-inspired 'bombshell' in contemporary democratic theory, establishing the book's provocative stakes through Mill's metaphor.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Understanding why electoral majorities today act in ways that threaten democratic foundations

theoretical_positioning.description: The review engages sympathetically with Tuck's Rousseauian majoritarianism while pressing an immanent critique about the undertheorised cultural conditions for democratic unity.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: close reading of Rousseau's texts and Tuck's argument
epistemic_mode.notes: Book review genre — argument proceeds through textual interpretation of Tuck and Rousseau, raising a normative/theoretical lacuna (cultural conditions of democracy)

case_type.case_name: Tuck, Active and Passive Citizens (2024); Rousseau, The Social Contract

confidence_notes: Book review genre is a clear case: the 'profile' reflects the reviewer's argumentative intervention (immanent critique of Tuck's omission of cultural conditions), not a standalone research paper. Move sequence maps the review's own rhetorical logic, not Tuck's book.

title: Rethinking political thinkers [Book Review]
author: Sakurai

opening_move.description: The review opens by mapping the disciplinary landscape of global/comparative political theory, then locates the reviewed volume's place (and limits) within it.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Whether PT can genuinely broaden beyond its Western, Anglophone, male-dominated canon

theoretical_positioning.description: The review performs an immanent critique: accepting the editors' decolonisation premise, but showing that language parochialism undercuts it, and arguing for genuine CPT as a more consistent alternative.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: close reading of the volume's introduction and table of contents
epistemic_mode.notes: Argument proceeds entirely through normative and methodological critique; no empirical data deployed.

case_type.case_name: Ramgotra and Choat (eds.), Rethinking Political Thinkers (2024)

normative_stake.domain: methodology / disciplinary politics of political theory

confidence_notes: Book review: reviewer's own argument is a methodological critique. The 'fully_normative' classification reflects the reviewer's own methodological stance, not empirical engagement. The move sequence captures the review's argumentative logic.

title: Normative sociology and normative behaviorism: recent discussions of the relation between empirical social science and normative political theory
author: Lægaard

opening_move.description: The paper opens by diagnosing a 'methodological moment' in political theory: while the strict separation of facts and values has been widely challenged, the precise implications of different alternatives for what political theorists should actually do remain underexplored.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Different views on the fact-value relation have concrete implications for what political theorists should do and which methods they should employ

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper conducts a systematic methodological comparison of two recent approaches without advocating for either; its own contribution is the analytical framework of the four comparison dimensions.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: conceptual analysis and systematic comparison of methodological positions
epistemic_mode.notes: The paper discusses empirical evidence as an object of methodological analysis but does not itself use any empirical data; it is a meta-methodological argument conducted entirely within conceptual/normative space.

confidence_notes: Unusual structure: both interlocutors (Floyd and Modood) are simultaneously 'central' and 'engaged_critically' since the paper subjects both to immanent critique without endorsing either. The paper's own epistemic mode is fully normative/conceptual despite its topic being about the empirical-normative interface.

title: Reconsidering cosmopolitanism and forgiveness: Arendt, Derrida, and 'care for the world' [Book Review]
author: de Jongh

opening_move.description: The review opens by situating Peys's book against a hostile political climate of xenophobia, framing it as a constructive contribution to rethinking cosmopolitan hospitality and political forgiveness.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Recovering the value of political forgiveness and cosmopolitan care in the face of contemporary dehumanising rhetoric

theoretical_positioning.description: The review accepts Peys's Arendtian-Derridean framework but presses an immanent critique: Peys reads Arendt's distinctions too sharply and underplays the material/labor dimension of worldly care.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: close reading of philosophical texts (Arendt, Derrida, Peys)
epistemic_mode.notes: Book review: argument is conducted through textual-philosophical interpretation; normative stake is the vision of cosmopolitan care for the world.

case_type.case_name: Peys, Reconsidering Cosmopolitanism and Forgiveness (2020); Arendt, The Human Condition; Derrida on forgiveness/hospitality

normative_stake.domain: cosmopolitanism / democratic theory / ethics of forgiveness

confidence_notes: Book review genre. The reviewer's own amendment — that labor/material care should be integrated into Peys's framework — is the substantive normative contribution. Move sequence maps the review's argumentative logic.

title: Friedrich Pollock: The éminence grise of the Frankfurt School [Book Review]
author: Kellner

opening_move.description: The review opens by identifying Pollock as a neglected figure in Frankfurt School historiography — the first book-length study of the Pollock–Horkheimer relationship fills a gap in intellectual history.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Recovering Pollock's foundational role in shaping the Marxist and critical theory orientation of the Frankfurt School

rivals_engaged: previous Frankfurt School histories that marginalised Pollock
theoretical_positioning.description: The review is broadly sympathetic to Lenhard's project, offering summary and contextualisation without a strong independent normative stance.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: intellectual history / genealogical reconstruction of Frankfurt School development
epistemic_mode.notes: Book review of an intellectual biography; the argument is primarily expository and contextualising rather than normatively interventionist.

case_type.case_name: Frankfurt School Institute for Social Research; Friedrich Pollock's career

confidence_notes: Book review genre; largely expository. Low normative stake reflects reviewer's descriptive-appreciative stance rather than argumentative intervention.

title: The political theory of liberal socialism [Book Review]
author: Maiwald

opening_move.description: The review opens by framing McManus's project as a 'retrieval' — a historically-grounded reconstruction of the liberal-socialist tradition for contemporary progressive use.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Recovering and making usable the liberal-socialist tradition for contemporary theoretical and political projects

theoretical_positioning.description: The review is sympathetic and primarily expository, contextualising McManus's synthesis of liberal-socialist thinkers without strong independent critique.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: ideological and intellectual history; close reading of the liberal-socialist canon
epistemic_mode.notes: Book review: the argument is a presentation and modest endorsement of McManus's reconstructive normative project.

title: Equality: what it means and why it matters [Book Review]
author: McManus

opening_move.description: The review opens with a polemical diagnosis of contemporary democratic crisis — the 2024 US election as symptom of entrenched oligarchy — to frame the reviewed book's stakes.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Understanding what equality means in an era of intensifying plutocracy and meritocratic ideology

theoretical_positioning.description: The review endorses the Piketty-Sandel dialogue but presses for a more radical structural critique that goes beyond the conversation's reformism.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: close reading of the Piketty-Sandel dialogue, with political-diagnostic framing
epistemic_mode.notes: Book review with strongly polemical political framing. The reviewer's own normative commitments (socialist egalitarianism) shape the critical assessment.

case_type.case_name: 2024 US Presidential election; Piketty & Sandel, Equality (2025)

confidence_notes: Short book review (3 pages) with unusually strong political framing. Reviewer is Matthew McManus — same person whose book was reviewed in 00756-x — lending the review a distinctive ideological voice.

title: Race still matters: the (post) racial politics of conspiracy theories
author: Ghumkhor, Gillespie, McMorrow

opening_move.description: The paper opens by diagnosing the proliferation of racist conspiracy theories (Great Replacement, White Genocide, QAnon, Eurabia) in liberal-democratic societies as a phenomenon that standard 'post-truth' explanations fail to account for.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: The dominant post-truth explanation reproduces a postracial obfuscation that conceals the structural-racial conditions enabling conspiracy theories in liberal democracies

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper argues against both post-truth and postracial frameworks, proposing a Lacanian psychoanalytic reading of conspiracy theories as symptoms of a repressed racial contract structuring liberal democracy.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Lacanian psychoanalytic theory; critical race theory; postcolonial analysis; conceptual analysis of conspiracy theories
epistemic_mode.notes: Despite the subject matter (empirical phenomena: conspiracy theories, racial politics), the argument is entirely theoretical-interpretive. Examples (QAnon, Great Replacement) are used illustratively to instantiate the theoretical framework, not as systematic empirical evidence.

case_type.case_name: QAnon, Great Replacement Theory, White Genocide, Eurabia conspiracy theories

confidence_notes: The paper uses conspiracy theories as illustrative examples to make a theoretical claim about the racial structure of liberal democracy. The epistemic mode is normative-interpretive, not empirical, despite engaging empirical phenomena.

title: Contesting the far right: a psychoanalytic and feminist critical theory approach [Book Review]
author: Travis

opening_move.description: The review opens with the central question driving Leeb's book: why do citizens turn to hateful, racist politics that run against their own interests? This frames the book as a response to the explanatory gap in far-right studies.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Providing a multi-dimensional theoretical framework to understand far-right radicalization beyond either economic or pathological explanations

rivals_engaged: pathologising approaches to the far right
theoretical_positioning.description: The review is appreciative and expository, endorsing Leeb's synthesis of psychoanalysis, critical theory, and decolonial theory for understanding far-right politics.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: psychoanalytic and critical theory reading of far-right politics
epistemic_mode.notes: Book review; primarily expository. The review presents and contextualises Leeb's theoretical framework.

confidence_notes: Book review genre; largely expository and appreciative. Normative stake is medium because the review endorses Leeb's critical-diagnostic project without adding an independent argument.

title: Civic education in polarized times [Book Review]
author: Stitzlein

opening_move.description: The review opens by situating the collection against the 'pressing problem of polarization' and the increasing importance of civic education to address it.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Polarization threatens democratic life; civic education is both more important and more difficult in this context

theoretical_positioning.description: The review evaluates the collection's contributions, endorsing the dialogue format and Driver's approach while raising Morton's critique about imaginative limitation.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: close reading and evaluation of contributors' proposals for civic education

title: The late Marx's revolutionary roads: colonialism, gender, and indigenous communism [Book Review]
author: Wapeemukwa

opening_move.description: The review opens by situating Anderson's book as recovering Marx's neglected post-1868 writings, arguing against scholars who dismiss this period and establishing its significance for understanding Marx's revised theory of revolution.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Marx's final years show a fundamental revision of his theory of revolution, race, and gender — recovering this corrects an Eurocentric and teleological reading of Marxism

rivals_engaged: Stedman-Jones and others who dismiss late Marx
theoretical_positioning.description: The review endorses Anderson's recovery of late Marx while pressing an immanent critique: Anderson's use of 'dialectic' risks abstracting Haitian revolutionary history into a theoretical model.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: textual and intellectual history; recovery of Marx's late notebooks
epistemic_mode.notes: Book review; primarily expository with a focused immanent critique about the abstraction of Haiti from its own intellectual-political history.

case_type.case_name: Marx's late notebooks; Haitian revolution (Saint-Domingue 1685-1861)

title: Race, time, and utopia: critical theory and the process of emancipation [Book Review]
author: Battaglini

opening_move.description: The review opens by presenting Paris's central claim: utopia functions as a critical theory in Black political thought to break with racialized temporal domination and inaugurate new social possibilities.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Diagnosing and conceptualising racial injustice as temporal domination in order to formulate emancipatory resources

rivals_engaged: pure structural accounts of racial justice
theoretical_positioning.description: The review is appreciative and expository, presenting Paris's two innovations (utopia as 'not-yet'; racial injustice as temporal domination) and contextualising their theoretical contributions.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: close reading of Paris and critical theory tradition; Du Bois exegesis
epistemic_mode.notes: Book review genre; largely expository. The normative stake belongs to the reviewed book's argument (utopian emancipation from racial domination).

title: The two faces of democracy: decentering agonism and deliberation [Book Review]
author: Rozenburg

opening_move.description: The review opens by affirming the need to bridge deliberative and agonist theories of democracy, positioning Scudder and White's book as an attempt to hold both legitimate democratic modes simultaneously.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Democratic theory needs to account for both legitimacy-through-reason and conflict-through-struggle; neither model alone is sufficient

theoretical_positioning.description: The review argues that Scudder and White's 'communicative model' ultimately fails to integrate the two models, and offers an independent diagnosis of why the synthesis is unsuccessful.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: conceptual analysis of deliberative and agonist models
epistemic_mode.notes: Book review; the reviewer's own position (that the synthesis fails) is the substantive contribution. The argument is conducted entirely within conceptual-normative space.

case_type.case_name: Scudder and White, The Two Faces of Democracy (2023)

title: Subaltern silence: A postcolonial genealogy [Book Review]
author: Mendez, Oberprantacher

opening_move.description: The review opens with Olson's concluding claim that subaltern silence is ubiquitous in the contemporary world, using Mbembe's 'Becoming Black' to establish the stakes: subalternity is not an elsewhere but a present condition.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: How to articulate silence without muting it — the paradox of speaking for/with subalterns without ventriloquism

rivals_engaged: abstraction of Haiti into theoretical model
theoretical_positioning.description: The review endorses Olson's genealogical project but raises a tension: his focus on Haiti-as-paradigm risks abstracting a specific historical-political site into a general theoretical model, suppressing its own epistemic complexity.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: postcolonial genealogy; close reading of Olson and Spivak
epistemic_mode.notes: Book review genre. The central tension raised (paradigm case vs. dynamic historical site) is the reviewers' independent critical contribution.

case_type.case_name: Saint-Domingue/Haiti (1685-1861); Olson, Subaltern Silence (2024)

confidence_notes: Book review genre with substantive immanent critique. The reviewers' concern about theorising from Haiti is the distinctive contribution.

title: Imagining democratic insurgency: enthusiasm, sublimity, and finitude
author: White

opening_move.description: The paper diagnoses a gap in accounts of sublimity in political theory: recent aesthetico-affective approaches treat sublimity as a dissensual, disruptive force, but neglect an older tradition linking sublimity to democratic collective binding — and neither tradition can adequately distinguish democratic from fascist enthusiasm.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Without a 'bivalent sublimity' encompassing both enthusiasm and sobering finitude, democratic insurgency cannot be distinguished from proto-fascist collective energism

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper engages Connolly's political aesthetics in a dual mode — critical of the undertheorised distinction between democratic and fascist enthusiasm, but constructive in drawing on Connolly's identity/difference ontology to build a 'sobering sublimity' out of finitude.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: close reading of Kant, Connolly, Burke, Sorel, Nietzsche; political-aesthetic analysis; 18th-century intellectual history
epistemic_mode.notes: Uses historical/philosophical cases (Robespierre, fascist aesthetics, Msezane's performance art, MLK's letter from Birmingham) as illustrative interpretive material, not systematic evidence.

case_type.case_name: Connolly's myth of the general strike; fascist rally aesthetics; Msezane's performance art; MLK's Letter from Birmingham Jail

confidence_notes: Full-length research article (19 pages). The argument is fully normative-interpretive: no empirical data but extensive philosophical case analysis. The 'interpretive case' designation reflects use of historical examples as theoretical lenses rather than constitutive evidence.

title: New perspectives on Judith Shklar [Critical Exchange]
author: Buxton, Bagg, Enoch, Marriott, Moyn

opening_move.description: The exchange opens by identifying Shklar as an underappreciated figure whose 'negative method' is increasingly attracting scholarly attention, and framing the five contributions as advancing new applications of her thought.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Recovering and extending the political relevance of Shklar's negativism — for radical politics, analytic philosophy, emotional response, and the Mill connection

theoretical_positioning.description: A multi-author critical exchange: each contributor reconstructs a different facet of Shklar's thought for contemporary use, collectively arguing for the enduring relevance of her negativist liberalism.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: close reading of Shklar's texts; intellectual-historical contextualisation
epistemic_mode.notes: Critical exchange format: 5 essays on Shklar, covering radical politics (Bagg), analytic engagement (Enoch), emotional hermeneutics (Marriott), and the Mill connection (Moyn), framed by Buxton's introduction.

case_type.case_name: Shklar's Liberalism of Fear (1989); Legalism (1964); American Citizenship lectures (1991)

confidence_notes: Multi-author critical exchange (27 pages). Treated as a single profile since all contributions are organised around extending Shklar's thought. The 'fully_normative' classification reflects the dominant mode across all five pieces.

title: Democracy despite itself: liberal constitutionalism and militant democracy [Book Review]
author: Sajó

opening_move.description: The review opens with the tension at the heart of militant democracy: measures that restrict democratic choices in order to defend democracy risk turning democracy into tyranny by democratic means.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Justifying militant democracy requires resolving the paradox that defensive restrictions of freedom may undermine the freedom they aim to protect

theoretical_positioning.description: The review endorses the need for militant democracy justification but raises the problem of identification: how to trigger militant democracy before democratic essentials are already subverted, as Hungary/PiS/Fidesz show.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: close reading of Schupmann; political analysis of Hungary, India, Turkey, Poland
epistemic_mode.notes: Book review with substantive immanent critique. Contemporary political examples (Hungary, Poland, Turkey) used to illustrate the identification problem in militant democracy.

case_type.case_name: Hungary (Fidesz), Poland (PiS), Turkey, India; Schupmann, Democracy Despite Itself (2024)

confidence_notes: Book review genre with strong political examples. The reviewer (Sajó, Democracy Institute, CEU) brings expert knowledge of Hungarian and Eastern European democratic backsliding that inflects the critique.

title: What remains: the collected poems of Hannah Arendt [Book Review]
author: Tang

opening_move.description: The review opens by asking who Arendt's inner interlocutors were and in what language they spoke — using the multilingual, itinerant quality of her poetry to locate a 'poetic canvas' of her political thinking.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Arendt's poems reveal a dimension of her political thought that cannot be recovered from her philosophical prose alone — the poetic thinking that underlies her pluralism

theoretical_positioning.description: The review is broadly appreciative and interpretive, presenting the collection as revealing an Arendt at the intersection of poetic tradition and political thought, without substantial critique.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: literary and philosophical hermeneutics; close reading of Arendt's poetry and prose
epistemic_mode.notes: Book review genre; predominantly literary-interpretive. The normative argument (if any) is implicit: recovering the full Arendt enriches our understanding of political pluralism.

title: The anarchist before the law: law without authority [Book Review]
author: Marneros

opening_move.description: The review opens by noting the intersection of law and anarchism as a growing scholarly field, positioning Newman and La Torre's book as offering a different and more productive account than the destituting anarchism-vs-law framing.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Law need not be the enemy of anarchism — anarchism's instituting power can produce an anti-foundational law as a practice of freedom

theoretical_positioning.description: The review is appreciative and expository, presenting the productive tension between Newman's continental anarchism and La Torre's analytic legal theory as the book's central strength.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: conceptual analysis of anarchist and legal traditions; close reading of Newman and La Torre
epistemic_mode.notes: Book review genre; primarily expository and appreciative.

title: Free speech and social change: a public reason approach
author: Bonotti, Nwokora

opening_move.description: The paper opens by diagnosing a legitimacy crisis for free speech in contemporary liberal democracies: social changes (individualism, migration, post-truth, cognitive biases, technology) have altered the empirical context in which the three classical arguments for free speech were developed, requiring their re-evaluation.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: The protection of free speech must be re-legitimized through a 'public reason test' that accounts for social change; without this, free speech protections lose their democratic justification

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper conducts a systematic re-evaluation of the three main arguments for free speech through the lens of Rawlsian public reason, arguing that only the democracy argument survives the test of contemporary social change.

epistemic_mode.qualitative_methods: qualitative analysis of social change processes (individualism, migration, post-truth, cognitive bias research, technological change)
epistemic_mode.notes: The paper uses empirical descriptions of social change processes to assess the viability of normative arguments — empirical material is supportive but not constitutive of the normative conclusion.

normative_stake.domain: democratic theory / free speech / constitutional law

confidence_notes: Full-length article (21 pages). The 'normative_with_qualitative_empirical' classification reflects the paper's use of social science descriptions of five change processes as inputs to the normative argument; the argument itself is normative-analytical.

title: Legalizing the revolution: India and the constitution of the postcolony [Book Review]
author: Marwah

opening_move.description: The review opens with the provocative thesis of Dasgupta's book: the postcolonial revolution failed, and its failure is legible in the constitutional project that converted mass democratic energy into top-down bureaucratic state-building.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Understanding how the constitutional project contained rather than channelled popular sovereignty in India, and why the anticolonial promise was domesticated

theoretical_positioning.description: The review is appreciative and expository, presenting Dasgupta's critical-genealogical argument about the containment of popular sovereignty in Indian constitutional history.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: intellectual and legal history; genealogical analysis of constitutional design
epistemic_mode.notes: Book review of a historical-political theory work. Dasgupta's book itself uses archival and historical evidence; the review presents this without independent empirical engagement.

title: Nancy Fraser and politics [Book Review]
author: Boscov-Ellen

opening_move.description: The review opens with a sweeping diagnosis of contemporary polycrisis (inequality, ecological collapse, displacement, xenophobia), framing Fraser's critical theory as uniquely equipped to trace 'heterogeneous ills' to a common capitalist root.

title: Contested territory: a theory of land and democracy beyond sovereign bounds [Book Review]

epistemic_mode.notes: Book review genre; primarily expository and appreciative.

title: In the shadow of minority rights: decolonising gender, liberalism and the politics of difference [Book Review]
author: Rodrigues

opening_move.description: The review opens by framing Dutta's book as a reframing of political agency in contemporary India: Muslim women navigate a landscape shaped by legal pluralism and communal politics through embodied ethical practice, not merely through liberal state claims.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: The liberal state is not the locus of justice for Muslim women in India; their political agency is enacted through everyday ethical practices within lived Islamic and constitutional traditions

theoretical_positioning.description: The review presents Dutta's postcolonial feminist argument appreciatively, foregrounding the book's empirical grounding in fieldwork with Muslim women activists.

epistemic_mode.notes: Book review of an empirically-grounded work. The reviewed book's fieldwork is constitutive of its argument; the review presents this without independent empirical contribution.

title: A critique of the polarisation narrative: expanding the limits of democracy, parties and political participation
author: Roch, Balinhas, Mondon

opening_move.description: The paper opens by diagnosing the 'polarisation narrative' as a discursive construction that, like the populism hype before it, shapes political understanding while obscuring real structural trends — specifically, it constructs a spectrum where legitimacy resides in the 'centre' and excludes democratic demands from the 'extremes'.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: The polarisation narrative legitimises technocratic post-political management and forecloses possibilities for democratic expansion; a critique of the narrative is needed to recover democratic alternatives

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper performs a critical discourse analysis of the polarisation narrative and proposes democratic expansion as an alternative framework, drawing on agonistic, Gramscian, and participatory democratic theory.

epistemic_mode.quantitative_methods: content analysis of NYT articles (Nexis search); frequency counts of 'polarisation' in academic publications
epistemic_mode.qualitative_methods: critical discourse analysis of the polarisation narrative
epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: discourse analysis; Gramscian hegemony theory
epistemic_mode.notes: The empirical data (media/publication counts) are supportive illustrations of the narrative's construction and growth, not constitutive of the normative argument for democratic expansion.

case_type.case_name: Trump's political rise and the polarisation discourse in the United States; European far-right mainstreaming

normative_stake.domain: democratic theory / political parties / public sphere

title: Facing authority: a theory of political legitimacy [Book Review]
author: Hyde, Saracino

opening_move.description: The review opens by situating Fossen's book within non-ideal theory, framing political legitimacy as a pressing practical question in conditions of institutional fragility and democratic backsliding.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: In conditions of turbulence, we need practical accounts of how to face authority when justice and consensus are precarious

theoretical_positioning.description: The review presents Fossen's 'facing authority' approach appreciatively — legitimacy as a practical orientation toward authority rather than an abstract concept — without substantial independent critique.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: conceptual analysis of political legitimacy
epistemic_mode.notes: Book review genre; primarily expository.

title: Ambedkar's political philosophy: a grammar of public life from the social margins [Book Review]
author: Jha

opening_move.description: The review opens by diagnosing a 'thin interpretation' problem in Ambedkar studies: existing literature is biographical or historical rather than analytically philosophical, and relies on a narrow canon of his texts.

title: Normative reconstructions of property rights in German classical philosophy [Review Essay]
author: Ovcharuk

Two recent systematic reconstructions reinstate these philosophers as critics of property's perennial political problem.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: A German idealist reconstruction of property theory offers normative resources beyond liberal rights-based and utilitarian accounts — recognition and pluralism as alternative foundations

title: Reframing restitution with Roberto Esposito and Achille Mbembe: restitutive justice struggles and the colonial 'dispositif of a thing'
author: Zolkos

opening_move.description: The paper opens by identifying a discrepancy between the international legal discourse of cultural property (which frames heritage objects as inanimate things subject to ownership rights) and the 'refractory speech acts' of activists and Indigenous spokespersons in restitutive justice struggles, who deploy richer, relational conceptions of thinghood. This discrepancy opens the gap that Esposito and Mbembe are recruited to theorise.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Restitutive justice struggles are decolonizing practices that challenge the binary separation between persons and things — a richer and more politically radical understanding of restitution beyond property relations and transfer of ownership

rivals_engaged: international legal discourse of cultural property (UNESCO/UNIDROIT)
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper synthesises Esposito's theory of the 'dispositif of a thing' and Mbembe's postcolonial theory of African objects to produce a decolonial critique of western museum discourse and a richer account of restitution that goes beyond legal ownership. The refractory speech acts of activists constitute the empirical-interpretive case.

epistemic_mode.qualitative_methods: analysis of activist speech acts; discourse analysis of restitution debates
data_sources: activist and artist statements in restitution debates
epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: conceptual analysis using Esposito and Mbembe; postcolonial theory; critical analysis of legal frameworks
epistemic_mode.notes: Full-length article (20 pages). The activist speech acts and restitution debates function as interpretive cases that are constitutive of the argument — the paper cannot be understood without them — but the core argument is normative-theoretical about what restitution should mean. Classified as normative_with_interpretive_case: the cases generate the theory but the paper's primary output is normative.

case_type.case_name: Restitutive justice struggles; activist 'refractory speech acts'; specific examples including Victoria Hunt's art and African heritage restitution campaigns

confidence_notes: Full-length article (20 pages). The activist speech acts are on the boundary between illustrative and constitutive evidence — classified as illustrative because the theoretical framework (Esposito + Mbembe) could in principle stand independently of the specific cases.

title: Hans Jonas and Sophocles on the ethics of the Anthropocene
author: Folit-Weinberg and van 't Klooster

opening_move.description: The paper opens with the 'Anthropocene question': what, if anything, is genuinely new about the ethical demands of the Anthropocene? It argues that only an ethics that answers this question precisely is properly an ethics of the Anthropocene — identifying a gap in normative theory that environmental humanities scholars address descriptively but not in terms of normative principles.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: An ethics of the Anthropocene must specify not only principles for action but principles for how to deliberate — and 5th-century Athenian tragedy provides exactly this, refuting both Jonas's anti-democratic pessimism and claims that democracy is unfit for the Anthropocene

rivals_engaged: Jonas's ethics of responsibility (Imperative of Responsibility 1979)
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper performs an immanent critique of Jonas — accepting his framework of historical novelty diagnosis while challenging his misdiagnosis of that novelty — and recruits Sophoclean tragedy as an alternative model for democratic deliberation under uncertainty. The synthesis argues that tragedy is a democratic practice of the sort the Anthropocene requires.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: close reading of Jonas and Sophocles; classical philology; normative political theory; intellectual history of Athenian democracy
epistemic_mode.notes: Full-length article (21 pages). Sophoclean tragedy is the primary interpretive case — deployed to challenge Jonas's claim that democracy is unfit for the Anthropocene. The case is constitutive of the argument in that the normative conclusion (democratic deliberation through tragedy) cannot be reached without it.

case_type.case_name: Jonas, The Imperative of Responsibility (1979); Sophocles, Athenian tragedy (5th century BCE)

normative_stake.domain: Anthropocene ethics / democracy / deliberation / environmental politics

confidence_notes: Full-length article (21 pages). The joint use of Jonas (normative theory) and Sophocles (classical case) is methodologically unusual — interdisciplinary between classics and political theory, as explicitly stated. The Sophocles case is constitutive, not merely illustrative.

title: The political aesthetics of cruelty: contemporary authoritarian politics and the struggle between sovereignty and relationality
author: Sabsay

opening_move.description: The paper opens with an urgent diagnosis of neo-authoritarian governments worldwide (Trump, Milei, Bukele, Orbán, Meloni, Erdoğan, Modi) as constituting a 'global historical moment obsessed with hostility and destruction' — a political aesthetics of cruelty. The opening directly frames the contemporary as a crisis demanding theoretical response.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Contemporary neo-authoritarianism is best understood through the concept of a 'political aesthetics of cruelty' — a political capture of the death drive — and vulnerability as relational existence offers the resources to contest it

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper develops an original conceptual framework — political aesthetics of cruelty, death drive, vulnerability as relationality — drawing on Derrida, Freud, and Butler to theorise neo-authoritarianism's psychic-political logic. This is not an immanent critique of rivals but a positive conceptual contribution.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: psychoanalytic political theory; Derridean deconstruction; feminist vulnerability theory; analysis of political aesthetics
epistemic_mode.notes: Full-length research article (19 pages). The neo-authoritarian governments named in the opening are illustrative cases for the theoretical framework. The core argument is normative-conceptual, drawing on Freud's death drive, Derrida's analysis of sovereignty and cruelty, and Butler's vulnerability theory.

case_type.case_name: Contemporary neo-authoritarian governments: Trump (USA), Milei (Argentina), Bukele (El Salvador), Orbán (Hungary), Meloni (Italy), Erdoğan (Turkey), Modi (India)

confidence_notes: Full-length research article (19 pages). The use of psychoanalytic concepts (death drive) for political theory is methodologically distinctive in this corpus. The contemporary authoritarian cases are illustrative but generate the theoretical question — hence 'illustrative_and_generative'. Conclusion is qualified/open rather than fully prescriptive.
